description: Boiled yam served alongside a rich, savoury tomato and egg stew — a classic Nigerian breakfast and comfort meal that comes together in under 30 minutes.

master.description: Achieve an S rating on the Beginner path to earn the Master achievement
master.display: Quiet permanent stamp on the path card

dish_legend.description: Achieve an S rating on the Beginner path — the only and highest path for this dish
dish_legend.display: Permanent mark on the dish card in the pack browser

beginner.description: Perfectly boiled yam — soft through but not falling apart — with a rich tomato and egg sauce that is well-seasoned and slightly bold.
prep_notes: The yam and egg sauce cook at roughly the same speed — start the yam first then make the sauce while it boils
White yam (the most common variety) is what you want — not water yam or yellow yam, both of which behave differently
Yam cooking time depends significantly on the thickness of the slices and the age of the yam — older yams take longer

ingredients.name: white yam (Nigerian yam)
ingredients.note: Peel thickly — the outer layer just under the skin can be bitter. Cut into slices about 3–4cm thick for even cooking.

ingredients.name: eggs
ingredients.note: Beat lightly with a pinch of salt before cooking.

ingredients.name: fresh plum tomatoes
ingredients.note: Blended gives a smoother sauce. Finely chopped gives a chunkier texture. Either works.

ingredients.name: red bell pepper (tatashe)
ingredients.quantity: half a medium, blended or finely chopped
ingredients.note: Adds sweetness and depth to the sauce. Skip if unavailable.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet or fresh pepper
ingredients.note: Blend with the tomatoes or chop finely. Skip for a mild sauce.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: Half finely chopped for the egg sauce. The other half sliced and added to the yam boiling water for flavour.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: For frying the tomato base of the egg sauce.

ingredients.name: seasoning cube (Maggi or Knorr)
ingredients.note: Crush to powder before adding to the sauce.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season the yam boiling water generously. Season the sauce to taste.

ingredients.name: green onion (spring onion)
ingredients.note: Added at the very end of the egg sauce for freshness.

steps.title: Peel and Boil the Yam
steps.instruction: Peel the yam thickly — remove a generous layer so no skin or tough outer layer remains. Cut into slices about 3–4cm thick. Place the slices in a pot, add the sliced half onion, and cover with cold water. Add a generous pinch of salt to the water. Bring to a boil on medium-high heat then reduce to a steady simmer. Cook until the yam is fully tender when pierced with a fork — about 15–25 minutes depending on thickness.
micro_steps: Peel the yam thickly — use a knife not a peeler, remove a generous layer
Rinse the peeled yam under cold water
Cut into slices about 3–4cm thick — consistent thickness helps them cook evenly
Place the yam slices in a pot
Add the sliced half onion — this adds flavour to the yam as it cooks
Cover with cold water — the water should sit about 2cm above the yam
Add a generous pinch of salt to the water
Bring to a boil on medium-high heat
Reduce to a steady simmer once boiling
Cook for 15–25 minutes
After 15 minutes pierce a thick slice with a fork — it should go through with some resistance
Continue cooking until the fork goes through cleanly with no resistance
Do not overcook — the yam should hold its shape not fall apart
steps.sensory_cue: The yam is ready when a fork slides through a thick slice cleanly with no resistance. The flesh should look white and opaque throughout — not translucent or grey in the centre. The water should smell slightly sweet and earthy from the yam.
steps.failure_risk: Undercooked yam is chalky and starchy in the centre — unpleasant to eat. Always test with a fork before removing from the heat. Overcooked yam falls apart completely and becomes waterlogged. The window between perfectly cooked and overcooked is about 3–5 minutes so check regularly after the 15-minute mark.

checkpoints.message: 15 minutes done. Test the yam with a fork now — does it go through with some resistance? Still firm? Give it 5 more minutes then test again.
Fork going through cleanly with no resistance? Yam is done — remove from heat and drain. If not done keep simmering and test every 3 minutes.

steps.title: Blend and Fry the Tomato Base
steps.instruction: While the yam is boiling make the sauce. Blend the tomatoes, red bell pepper, scotch bonnet, and the finely chopped half onion until smooth — no water needed. Heat the vegetable oil in a pan on medium heat. Pour in the blended mix and fry, stirring every minute, for 6–8 minutes until the sauce thickens, darkens slightly, and the raw tomato sharpness is gone.
micro_steps: Add the tomatoes, red bell pepper, scotch bonnet, and finely chopped half onion to the blender
Blend until smooth — no water needed
Heat 3 tablespoons of vegetable oil in a wide pan on medium heat
Pour in the blended tomato mix — stand back slightly, it may splatter
Stir immediately to combine with the oil
Fry on medium heat, stirring every minute
Fry for 6–8 minutes until the sauce is noticeably thicker and darker
The raw tomato sharpness should be completely gone from the smell
The oil should just be starting to separate at the surface
steps.sensory_cue: The tomato base should look thicker and darker than when it went in — a rich dark orange-red. The raw tomato sharpness should be gone, replaced by a cooked, slightly sweet tomato smell. The oil should just be beginning to separate at the surface.
steps.failure_risk: Under-frying the tomato base leaves a raw sharp taste in the egg sauce. Give it the full 6–8 minutes even though it is a small quantity. The oil separating is the sign it is ready.

checkpoints.message: Sauce thickening and darkening? Raw tomato smell fading? Give it another 2–3 minutes to fully cook out.
Raw tomato smell completely gone? Oil just separating at the surface? Ready for the seasoning and eggs.

steps.title: Season the Sauce
steps.instruction: Add the crushed seasoning cube and a pinch of salt to the fried tomato base. Stir well and let it cook for 1 minute. Taste the sauce — it should be well-seasoned and slightly bold since the eggs will mellow it. Adjust with more salt if needed.
micro_steps: Add the crushed seasoning cube to the tomato base — stir in well
Add a pinch of salt — stir
Cook for 1 minute on medium heat
Taste the sauce carefully
It should taste well-seasoned and slightly bold — the eggs will absorb and mellow the flavour
Add more salt if it tastes flat
Confirm the seasoning before the eggs go in — it is easier to adjust now
steps.sensory_cue: The sauce should smell deeply savoury and rich. The taste should be bold and well-seasoned — not flat or bland.
steps.failure_risk: Under-seasoning at this stage is the most common mistake with egg sauce. The eggs absorb and dilute the seasoning — what tastes just right now will taste bland after the eggs go in. Season boldly.

checkpoints.message: Taste the sauce now. Bold and well-seasoned? Good — add the eggs. Flat? Add more salt before the eggs go in.

steps.title: Add Eggs and Finish the Sauce
steps.instruction: Reduce the heat to medium-low. Pour the beaten eggs directly into the tomato sauce and stir immediately and continuously for about 1 minute until the eggs are fully cooked through and distributed through the sauce in small pieces. Add the sliced spring onion. Taste one final time and adjust salt if needed. The egg sauce is done.
micro_steps: Reduce the heat to medium-low
Pour the beaten eggs into the tomato sauce
Stir immediately and continuously — do not let the egg set in a large lump
Keep stirring for about 60 seconds until the eggs are fully cooked
The egg should look like small cooked pieces distributed through the sauce
The sauce should look thicker and richer now with the egg cooked through
Add the sliced spring onion — stir once
Taste one final time — adjust salt if needed
Turn off the heat
steps.sensory_cue: The finished egg sauce should look rich, thick, and deeply coloured — orange-red from the tomatoes with visible pieces of cooked egg throughout. It should smell deeply savoury. The egg pieces should look fully cooked — no wet or runny parts.
steps.failure_risk: Pouring the eggs in without stirring immediately causes them to set in one large mass that is difficult to break up. Stir the moment the eggs hit the hot sauce and keep stirring until they are fully cooked and distributed.

checkpoints.message: Eggs fully cooked and distributed? Sauce looking thick and rich? Spring onion added? Taste one final time then serve with the yam.

steps.title: Drain the Yam and Serve
steps.instruction: Drain the cooked yam through a sieve or carefully pour off the water. Place the yam slices on a serving plate. Serve immediately alongside the egg sauce. The yam and sauce should hit the table at the same time — time your sauce to finish just as the yam is done.
micro_steps: Drain the cooked yam through a sieve or pour off the boiling water carefully
Place the yam slices on a serving plate
The yam should look white and opaque — soft through but holding its shape
Spoon the egg sauce generously alongside or on top of the yam
Serve immediately while both are hot
steps.sensory_cue: The boiled yam should look white and slightly glossy. It should smell clean and slightly earthy. When you press a slice gently with a fork it should yield easily with no resistance throughout.
steps.failure_risk: Leaving the cooked yam sitting in the hot water after it is done continues cooking it and it will become waterlogged and fall apart. Drain it as soon as it is cooked.

checkpoints.message: Yam drained and plated? Egg sauce ready? Serve immediately while everything is hot.
